segments.text: Hi, my name is Nicholas Arcolano and I'm
the head of research at Jellyfish. Here
at Jellyfish, we provide an engineering
intelligence platform that gives
software teams observability into how
they're adopting and succeeding with AI
tools across the software development
life cycle. I'm here to speak with you
about how we go beyond coding metrics
and what it looks like scaling AI for
real business outcomes. So, let's dig Key
in. We're going to talk about AI
transformation, specifically what we can
learn from real world data about what's
happening out in the wild and what you
should expect in your own organization.
A lot of AI native companies have been
founded in the last year. there's
more being founded every day and
there are many more existing companies
though. they're trying to transform
themselves into being AI native. I've
talked to folks from many of these
companies. They all have the same big
questions. They want to know things like
what does good adoption look like?
what do productivity gains look like as
you adopt AI coding tools and agents?
You know, what should you expect as you
transform?
What are some side effects of these
transformations? What other things are
going to happen as you change the way
that you work? And most importantly, if
AI transformation isn't working out, if
it's not delivering as advertised,
what's going on? And what can you do
about it? here at Jellyfish, we
believe that the best way to get answers
is with data. So, in the next 15 to 20
minutes, I'm going to give you some data
backed insights from some studies that
we've done to help you tackle these
big questions. The Jellyfish AI
Before we dive in though, I just
wanted to take a moment to plug our
jellyfish AI engineering trends site. If
you find the data that we talk about
here interesting, this is a resource
that you can go to dig in deeper to find
updated information about how all these
things are changing. Check out new
analyses. We're going to keep updating
this with new insights and
updated results. So, if you find
things here valuable, this is a
place that you can go. Data Sources:
All right, about the data. This is
going to underpin the rest of the
stuff in this talk. At Jellyfish, we
provide analytics and insights for
software engineering leaders. To do
this, we combine multiple different
sources of information together. The
first one, obviously, we're going to be
talking about AI coding tools, things
like Copilot, Cursor, and Claude Code.
We're pulling in behavioral data of how
engineers are using these tools
day-to-day.
There's also autonomous agents,
things like Codex, that are
running off on their own, PR review
bots, agents that that you're
introducing into your tool stack. We're
pulling data from these as well. And
then we're taking all of these AI
technology signals and we're combining
this data with things from source
control platform. So things like GitHub
that let you understand about the
actual codebase, what code is being
written, what code is being shipped.
We also pull in data from task
management platforms, things like
linear, Jira. This tells you about
how teams are structured, what
the actual goal of work is that's being
done, what's a bug,
things like that.
So for the rest of this talk, we're
going to be looking at findings from a
pretty large data set. We're talking
about 20 million pull requests worth of
data. So this represents about 200,000
developers from around about a
thousand companies.
All right, let's let's dig in.
question one, adoption. What does
good adoption look like? Well, let's
start with an easy one. Weekly active
users. This is one of the most basic
metrics and we're just measuring to what
extent folks have been successful just
rolling out AI tools to their org
getting people to adopt them in any way.
Here we're showing what the median
WAU is a fraction of total engineers at
the company looks like over time you
know across a set of companies. So you
can see the black line there is the
median. You can also see the 10th
25th 75th 90th percentiles. So you can
see across the entire distribution
adoption's been growing steadily over
the past year and now we're at a point
where the median company the black line
has about 63% of its engineers are using
AI coding tools on a weekly
basis.
Now, a more interesting metric is
I think to look at frequent AI users.
you know, what we're defining here is
frequent as engineers that are using AI
consistently, three or more days a week.
You know, maybe still a low bar, but
represents a dedicated
habit of using these tools regularly.
so it's important to to look at these
kinds of metrics because you want to
understand that that people are
establishing a habit of these tools
folding them into their workflow. This
is going to be a better measure of
adoption and something that we find is
more predictive of outcomes.
So what you're looking at here is a
stacked bar chart where now
we've put the companies into different
cohorts. the purple representing
companies that have 50% or more of their
engineers using AI coding tools
frequently. 25 to 50, 10 to 25, and
then the gray is less than 10% of
engineers.
So you can see here looking at the
purple bar, we're at a point now as of
January 2026 where more than half of
companies have more than half of their
engineers using these tools frequently.
So, you know, that's quite a bit of
growth you can see in this chart. Now,
if you're at a company in that purple
cohort, maybe you're like us,
at Jellyfish, you're way above
50%. You've got everybody on your
team using these tools, synchronous
and asynchronous agents, multiple
tools in parallel. So maybe it seems
crazy to you that not everybody is
operating at that level. But the reality
is that many teams still face real
barriers technical organizational
privacy and security barriers, cultural
barriers to adopting these tools more
completely. So you know for big or high
compliance situations roll out can be
challenging and we're seeing that in the
data.
This brings me to my final point on
adoption. Let's talk about autonomous
coding agents.
Now, the results we've talked about so
far, they're from
interactive coding tools. So, Copilot,
Cursor, Claude Code, things like that.
You know, we know that these tools
all have an interactive mode. You
know, they will write massive amounts of
code for you. Not just
autocomplete. But what about fully
autonomous agents? Things like Codex,
things where you give it a spec, you
give it a prompt, it goes off on its
own. It does all of the work.
You know,
a human might be approving the work, but
an agent does everything
up until that point. So, if you'd asked
me last year, how common this
is, you might hear a lot about it on,
X,
about the way that people
are using these agents, you know, in the
Silicon Valley arena,
but out in the wild, I would
have told you that was less than 1% of
what people were doing with AI coding
tools. But we've seen huge growth here
and rapid acceleration. So what
we're looking at now, this chart is
percent of PRs by company that have been
completed by an autonomous agent. So an
agent's done the coding totally by
itself based on a spec or a prompt.
Open up the PR, you know, a person
might have and most likely approved it,
but an agent did all the work. So the
black line, that's the median again.
We're still talking about,
basically nothing through most of last
year and now around 1% of pull requests
being completed by autonomous agents.
What's really interesting is those upper
percentile bands. So the P75, the
P90, you can see at those tails of the
distribution like the 90th percentile
top adopters here, this is exponential
growth. They've approached 10%
of their PRs as of January and
they're accelerating. So, we're seeing
rapid acceleration and adoption of these
ways of working, particularly the orgs
at in the tails of the distribution. You
know, the ones leading the charge here
are really starting to pull away from
the pack in their use of autonomous
agents.
All right, now let's talk about
productivity. even though you know
we're still talking largely interactive
coding agents and we're seeing some
growth in autonomous agents you know not
necessarily agent swarms at scale but
we are seeing some strong productivity
gains so let's talk about what we're
seeing first though productivity can
be kind of a squishy overloaded term
there's lots of ways to attack it so
what do we mean by productivity
Good place to start, plain old PR
throughput. How many pull requests
does the average engineer merge per
week? Not the most exotic metric, but
it's proven. It's widely accepted.
one
thing to know is that you know
the absolute level of PR throughput,
you know, is to a good number. that's
something that varies company
to company. It depends on things like
how you scope your work, how
big a PR is for you and your tech stack.
depends on your architecture.
actually we're going to talk about that
in a little bit. But all things being
equal for your org, measuring change in
PR throughput for your team is a good
way to track productivity gains.
another good one, cycle time. Lots of
ways to define cycle time, but here
we're going to talk about the time from
the first commit in a pull request until
that work was shipped till it was
merged.
So let's talk productivity. So here's
what we're seeing for changes in PR
throughput. And I'll explain this chart.
every data here, every data point
here is a snapshot of a given company on
a given week. So on the x-axis we
have a metric we call AI adoption
rate. This is related to the adoption
things I showed you earlier, but we've
normalized by coding days.

So you know when engineers
aren't active or on
vacation we can normalize out
that behavior. So think of it as
measuring the proportion of how much
engineers are using coding tools
relative to the amount of time that
they're writing code.
So that's the x-axis. The y-axis is
average PRs per engineer across
that company on that week. So every
week's company snapshot average PRs per
engineer. So you know size independent
for the company and then the x-axis is
AI adoption level. So you can see a
clear correlation here. and if you
fit a trend line, this average trend is
about a 2x change as you go from 0% to
100%. So on average, a company should
expect that as their engineers go from
not using these tools to using them
every time they code, that should
correspond to about a doubling of PR
throughput.
Now we also see some gains in cycle
times, which is interesting. This is
similar to the previous chart. every
point is a company snapshot on a given
week. Y-axis now is median cycle time
for all the work that was merged that
week and the x-axis is still level of
AI usage.
As an aside, I like this
distribution is kind of fun. You see
these two clear horizontal bands. So,
you know, the bottom cluster is going to
be all the things that have cycle times
that are about one workday in length.
You know, they take less than a workday
to ship. The second band above
it being things that take about two work
days. And then you can imagine there's a
big long tail of things I've cut off
the plot because there are things that
take many days or even weeks to ship,
but most tasks get completed within a
day or two is typical.
So again, there's an average trend here.
It corresponds to about a 24%
decrease in cycle times as you go from
0% to 100% adoption of AI coding tools.
So we're definitely seeing some
productivity gains.
Now you might ask, what else is
happening?
No such thing as a free lunch. What else
is going on? So, one thing we've
noticed is that pull requests are
definitely getting bigger. AI is writing
more code and it's writing bigger code.
Here's a plot like the previous one I
showed, except now the y-axis is
average additions, average additions per
PR. So size of the PR.
and

you see a trend here
that corresponds to as
you get to full AI adoption about an 18%
increase in the size of PRs. If you dig
in, you can look at additions and
deletions. This is primarily
additions is where we're seeing the new
code. So we're talking about AI
writing more net new code. It's not
necessarily just rewriting or heavily
reworking code on average. We also
didn't see any changes in the number of
files touched. So this change here is
more about code
the same files AI is not finding more
files to touch. But it really does
seem like code that's more verbose. It
might have more tests or exception
handling, more commenting. But it
really is trending towards
bigger pull requests.
Now, that's an interesting side effect.
I think a more pressing side effect
on a lot of people's mind is quality.
So, if teams are pushing more PRs and
merging them faster, and if the PRs
are getting bigger and maybe harder to
review, you might be wondering about
quality. So, there's lots of ways to
look at and define quality. We'll talk
about a few here, but there's many more
that we're looking at. So, for this
study, we've looked at bug
tickets created and we've looked at PR
reverts. So, as you use more AI
coding tools, are you just
generating more bugs? Are more bugs
being reported? And are you having to
roll back more code? Are more things
going out the door that have to be
undone because you're finding defects?
and the answer is we're not seeing a
ton right now. So we didn't see any
statistically significant relationship
with bug tickets created. We are
observing some small impacts to PR
revert. So we are seeing more code being
rolled back. It's small about a 7 to 11%
increase in revert rates over the
baseline. by which I mean all the
higher adoption AI cohorts relative
to little to no adoption you're
talking about on average about
bump in revert rates.
One interesting thing is we did see an
increase in the rate of bugs resolved.
So more bugs aren't being created but
more bugs are being resolved. And it
turns out that this is largely because
a lot of folks are pointing AI coding
tools at their bug backlogs
and burning those down
things that maybe weren't
high priority bugs but things that are
good well scoped tasks to point AI
coding tools and agents at and so we
are seeing some effects there. So, this
all adds up to there doesn't seem to be
a big smoking gun on quality yet. But
this is something that we're going
to keep an eye on because we're
starting to see some impacts and with
the acceleration of AI coding agents,
autonomous agents, and with the big
productivity gains and them continuing
to grow, we expect that you'll start to
see some pressures on other parts of the

When AI Productivity
Expectations Don’t Match Reality
All right. question number four,
last question. What if your expectations
don't match reality?
You know, what if you're trying to
go through AI transformation in your org
and you're still not getting the kinds
of results that we've been talking about
here. So, hopefully I've made it
clear that the most important thing to
focus on first is adoption. It's obvious
that you're not going to get gains
unless you get folks in your org using
these tools at scale. But maybe you're
doing that. Maybe you're seeing high
adoption. You're not seeing the kind of
productivity gains that you know you see
in your social networks. So what's
going on? We've looked at a lot of
things here. There's plenty more to
investigate. This is a very rich area.
But to wrap things up here, I'd like
to share one result that I think is
particularly interesting. And that's
code architecture. And I'll explain what
I mean by that. By code architecture
we're talking about how is the code for
your products and services organized
across your repositories.
So you want to think like
monorepo versus polyrepo monolith
versus microservices. How distributed is
your codebase and how is it organized?
We're going to look at a simple
metric for this. Obviously there's a lot
of complexity and a lot of different
models here. We're going to boil it all
down to one metric which is active repos
per engineer. This is pretty
straightforward. It just means how many
distinct repos does a typical engineer
push code to in a given week. So it's a
proxy for how much of the code base
does an engineer have to work with to
get things done.
So you know the nice thing about
this metric is you know I think it's
a nice way to summarize all of
these different architectural and
product choices. It also turns out
that it's scale independent. If you
compute this metric per engineer, it
doesn't actually correlate with the size
of the team. So as your company gets
bigger, you tend to get more repos, but
engineers tend to work with the same
number of repos because it's more a
function of how you've organized your
code and not necessarily how big your

company is. Software Architecture
Distribution Across Four Quartiles
All right. before we go further,
let's let me show you distribution of
what this looks like. So this is what
weekly active repositories per engineer.
So how many repos an engineer worked
with in a given week what it looks like
across all those companies.
So this curve that you see we've
broken it up into four quartiles. So
all the way on the left if you look at
this distribution you're talking about
the most centralized types of
architectures. So you're talking about
monorepo single product everything is
highly highly centralized and then as
you go to the right you see a big long
tail as things get more and more
distributed and in the orange at the
most highly distributed quartile
engineers may have to work with four
five or more repos in order to
do their job in a given week. And that
corresponds to things like you know when
you want to make a single feature change
that you have to push you know you
have to work with multiple microservices
or push migrations to multiple
different services.
So we're going to take these quartiles
these four different regions
centralized balanced distributed and
highly distributed and we are going to
look at what PR throughput changes
look like if we slice by these four
things. So you know remember this chart
from before. We're now going to have
four of these charts where instead of
every company snapshot you're going
to have company snapshots corresponding
to those four different architecture
regimes. And if you do that, it looks
like this. So now upper left here, we've
got your centralized architectures and
then we've got balanced and then on
the bottom left to right distributed and
highly distributed. So if you do that
same analysis, we see actually big
differences. So if you look at the
top row, we see that the centralized and
balanced code architectures, they have
productivity gains that trend more like
4x, not like 2x.
The distributed the lower left, those
look more like that global 2x trend. And
then what's highly interesting is that
highly distributed case where there's
essentially no correlation, it's even
slightly negative between AI adoption
and PR throughput. So, so what's going
on here? Why are teams with highly
distributed architectures struggling?
A big part of what you're seeing here is
the problem of context. So, most of
today's tools are really set up to best
work within one repo or one project at a
time. Combining context across repos is
typically challenging. It's challenging
for humans as well as agents. You
know you have to do a lot of research
hopping around switching branches.
Moreover even if there's a lot written
down in the code and it can be painful.
You could do it. But there's the
additional challenge that relationships
between these repos and systems the
way that your code architecture is
actually structured might not even be
written down clearly. It might be
largely locked in the heads of senior
engineers. It might be that people just
know that if you make a change here,
you've got to make a change over there.
You know your architecture diagram
might be out of date. So certainly
robots aren't going to have access to
these things to get the context they
need to make sense of this world. just
like a person has to go around and use
some insider trade
knowledge drawing on senior
expertise at your org to be able
to get things done. So what we're seeing
here ultimately is a problem of context.
As an aside another thing you might
notice is that these distributions
all shift upward across the board as we
go from more centralized to more
distributed. In other words,

highly distributed
architectures just need more
PRs to get the same amount of work done.
And this is because of things like
migrations and cross repo coordination.
So this is why I mentioned earlier that
counting PRs in the absolute sense isn't
a great metric. You need to be tracking
your own change in PR throughput to
understand productivity because this
varies a lot depending on these
factors.
Okay, so that's it. So to recap,
probably not news to anyone,
but AI coding tools are being used in a
big way. But autonomous agent use is
accelerating fast and we're seeing
strong growth there. We're also seeing
consistent productivity gains, more
code being shipped, it's being shipped
faster. And so you know even if
you're just using things like Copilot
or Cursor in a code completion maybe
some agentic ways you know you're
using Claude Code you know you should
start with a 2x change in your PR
throughput should be your baseline
expectation. There are going to be
some other things that change. For
instance bigger PRs quality may be an
issue. There's no alarm bells yet, but
that's a thing that we're all going to
want to keep an eye on. And there's lots
of reasons why your mileage may vary.
But one place to start is to think about
your own code architecture. And in
general, think about context
engineering. How do the humans know what
they need to know in order to make
sense of your code and your
architecture? And how are robots going
to know that to be able to do the
same kind of work? And so, you know, we
see some real differences depending on
the way you've chosen to structure your
code. And there's going to need to be
context engineering solutions to help
with that.
